local_names.romanized_local: Kunyit kering (Malay); Haldi ki jad (Hindi romanized)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers turmeric as a whole food ingredient; the dried root form is the closest defensible match. No dedicated dried-root subclass exists in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LQ covers dried turmeric rhizome under spices and herbs category; used as the closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227312002 refers to turmeric as a substance; no dried-root-specific code exists. Used as the closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of dried turmeric root is approximately 1 teaspoon (3g), used as a spice or flavouring in curries, rice dishes, and beverages.

nutrient_source.title: Turmeric, ground — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 02043)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10060023, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for turmeric, ground (FDC ID 172231) to validate macro and micronutrient plausibility. HPB values are used as primary; USDA used for secondary nutrient gap-filling where HPB does not report the field.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices and Condiments — Analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-GI spices and dried herbs; turmeric GI estimated at approximately 25 consistent with peer-reviewed literature (Bhatt et al., 2020, Journal of Functional Foods)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Journal of Functional Foods (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried turmeric root exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 25 is estimated based on: (1) very high dietary fibre content (22.7g/100g) which substantially attenuates glycaemic response; (2) moderate net carbohydrate content (44.4g total carbohydrate minus 22.7g fibre = 21.7g available carbohydrate per 100g); (3) peer-reviewed literature (Bhatt et al., 2020, Journal of Functional Foods) reporting curcumin's insulin-sensitising properties and low glycaemic impact of turmeric; (4) typical culinary serving of 3g contributes only 0.65g available carbohydrate, yielding GL of approximately 0.3. GI category: low (<55).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (27mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (2080mg/100g) with a sodium-to-potassium ratio strongly favouring blood pressure reduction
hypertension.guidance: Dried turmeric root is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension: its sodium content is negligible at culinary serving sizes (3g provides only 0.8mg sodium), while its potassium content (2080mg/100g) supports vasodilation; curcumin additionally exerts vasodilatory and endothelial-protective effects, and the high dietary fibre (22.7g/100g) contributes to blood pressure reduction through gut-mediated mechanisms.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment, low estimated GI (~25), and curcumin's evidence-based insulin-sensitising and hypoglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried turmeric root is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: a typical 3g serving contributes only 0.65g available carbohydrate and a glycaemic load of approximately 0.3; curcumin has been shown in peer-reviewed trials to improve insulin sensitivity, reduce HbA1c, and attenuate post-prandial glucose response; combining turmeric with black pepper (piperine) enhances curcumin bioavailability by up to 2000%, maximising these benefits.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory curcumin, high dietary fibre, very low saturated fat, and negligible cholesterol supporting cardiovascular risk reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried turmeric root is beneficial for cardiovascular health: curcumin inhibits NF-κB and reduces circulating inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6, TNF-alpha) implicated in atherosclerosis; the high dietary fibre (22.7g/100g) supports LDL cholesterol reduction; cholesterol is 0mg/100g; saturated fat is low at approximately 1g/100g; and the high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports arterial health — all consistent with a cardioprotective dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Curcumin directly inhibits NF-κB, TNF-alpha, and IL-6 inflammatory pathways central to RA pathogenesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried turmeric root is one of the most evidence-supported anti-inflammatory spices for rheumatoid arthritis: curcumin inhibits key inflammatory mediators (NF-κB, TNF-alpha, IL-6, COX-2) relevant to RA; bioavailability is substantially enhanced by co-administration with piperine (black pepper) and a dietary fat source; patients on anticoagulant therapy should consult their prescriber before using high-dose turmeric supplements, though culinary quantities (1–5g/day) are generally considered safe.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried turmeric root (Curcuma longa) is the dehydrated rhizome of the turmeric plant, a member of the ginger family (Zingiberaceae), widely used as a spice, colouring agent, and traditional medicine across South Asia and Southeast Asia. The dried root appears as hard, orange-yellow pieces or fingers with a rough, wrinkled surface; when ground, it produces the characteristic bright yellow-orange powder familiar in curries, rice dishes, and golden milk. Its flavour profile is earthy, slightly bitter, pungent, and mildly peppery, with a warm aromatic quality derived from its volatile oils (turmerone, atlantone, zingiberene) and the principal bioactive polyphenol curcumin. Nutritionally, dried turmeric root is notable for its very high dietary fibre content (22.7g/100g), moderate protein (9.7g/100g), low fat (3.2g/100g), and significant micronutrient density including exceptionally high potassium (2080mg/100g), iron (55mg/100g), calcium (168mg/100g), and magnesium. Its estimated glycaemic index is low (~25), and a typical culinary serving of 3g contributes negligible available carbohydrate and a glycaemic load of approximately 0.3. Clinically, curcumin — the primary bioactive compound — has been extensively studied for anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, insulin-sensitising, hypolipidaemic, and cardioprotective properties. It is particularly relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Curcumin bioavailability is low when consumed alone but is enhanced up to 2000% by co-administration with piperine (black pepper). Dried turmeric root is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is used across Indian, Malay, Indonesian, Thai, and Middle Eastern culinary traditions.

What nutrients are in dried turmeric root?
meal_context: Malay and Indonesian rendang and gulai

ingredient_synonyms.jiang huang: Chinese common name for turmeric (姜黄)
ingredient_synonyms.turmeric powder: ground form of dried turmeric root

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10060023, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172231) for secondary nutrient validation. GI is an analogous estimate supported by peer-reviewed literature (Bhatt et al., 2020) given absence of a direct GI measurement in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes are defensible approximations with documented gap notes. All hard gates pass.
notes: 1. Iron value of 55mg/100g sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (F10060023). This value is notably high; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reports 41.42mg/100g for turmeric ground. The HPB value is retained as primary per source hierarchy rules; the discrepancy is noted for reviewer attention. 2. Saturated fat (1.0g), monounsaturated fat (0.4g), polyunsaturated_fat (0.9g), trans_fat (0.0g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172231) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; documented as adapted values. 3. Vitamin C (25.9mg), B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172231) as secondary gap-fill; HPB does not report these fields. 4. Vitamin A (mcg RAE) set to -1: HPB does not report RAE form and USDA reports retinol activity equivalents inconsistently for turmeric; detailed RAE data not available in either primary source as of 2026. 5. Vitamin B12 (0.0mg) and Vitamin D (0.0mcg) are confirmed zero for a plant-based dried spice — naturally zero as turmeric is a plant-derived food containing no animal-sourced nutrients. 6. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g — naturally zero as turmeric is a plant food. 7. GI of approximately 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for dried turmeric root exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 8. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' in the input has been removed as dried turmeric root is a rhizome/root, not a grain; 'low_sodium' and 'high_fibre' are retained as accurate. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and saturated_fat_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions.
